title: How Plants Know Which Way Is Up

lesson.learn: Plants sense gravity and use it to orient roots downward and shoots upward. This response, called gravitropism, helps seedlings establish themselves and keeps mature plants growing in stable directions. Special cells inside plant tissues shift dense particles when the plant changes position. That movement redistributes growth hormones, especially auxin, so one side of the organ grows faster than the other and the plant bends into the correct orientation. Gravity is one of the quiet signals that shapes every plant. Without it, roots, stems, and leaves would be much less organized and far less effective at finding light, water, and support.
lesson.simpler: Plants can tell which way is down. Roots grow toward gravity, and shoots grow away from it.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Gravitropism lets plants orient themselves using gravity-driven hormone signals instead of nerves or muscles.
lesson.deeperDive:
Gravitropism and phototropism often work together. A stem may lean toward light while still preserving its overall sense of vertical growth, which is why plants look so purposeful even without a brain.

This response matters in agriculture, space biology, and seedling development. In microgravity, plants can still grow, but they lose a major cue that normally helps them establish roots and shoots correctly.
lesson.funFact: If you rotate a potted plant, its roots and shoots slowly correct themselves because the plant keeps re-reading gravity.

people: M. K. McSteen - Modern plant movement science
places: University physiology labs - Plant movement research
NASA research centers - Plant orientation work
years: 2020s - Plant movement is studied at cellular scale
concepts: Statoliths - Dense particles that help sense gravity
